# @joakimbugge/typeorm-seeder

> Decorator-based entity seeding for TypeORM. Annotate entity properties with `@Seed()` to describe how they should be populated, then create or persist fully populated entity graphs — including relations, embedded types, and circular guards — with a single call.

Full documentation: https://joakimbugge.github.io/seeders/guide/

## Installation

```bash
npm install @joakimbugge/typeorm-seeder typeorm reflect-metadata
```

Requires these `tsconfig.json` options:

```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "experimentalDecorators": true,
    "emitDecoratorMetadata": true
  }
}
```

## @Seed() decorator

Annotate entity properties with `@Seed()`. Scalar properties take a factory function; relation properties take the bare decorator or a `count` option.

```ts
import { Entity, PrimaryGeneratedColumn, Column, OneToMany, ManyToOne } from 'typeorm'
import { faker } from '@faker-js/faker'
import { Seed } from '@joakimbugge/typeorm-seeder'

@Entity()
class Author {
  @PrimaryGeneratedColumn()
  id!: number

  @Seed(() => faker.person.fullName())
  @Column()
  name!: string

  @Seed({ count: 3 })           // seed 3 related Book instances
  @OneToMany(() => Book, (b) => b.author)
  books!: Book[]
}

@Entity()
class Book {
  @Seed(() => faker.lorem.words(4))
  @Column()
  title!: string

  @Seed()                        // bare decorator on a to-one relation
  @ManyToOne(() => Author, (a) => a.books)
  author!: Author
}
```

### Sequence index

The factory receives a zero-based index as its third argument — counts up across `createMany`/`saveMany` batches:

```ts
@Seed((_, __, i) => `user-${i}@example.com`)
@Column()
email!: string
```

### Depending on earlier properties

Properties are seeded in declaration order. Each factory receives the partially-built entity as its second argument:

```ts
@Seed(() => faker.date.past())
@Column()
beginDate!: Date

@Seed((_, self: Event) => faker.date.future({ refDate: self.beginDate }))
@Column()
endDate!: Date
```

### Circular guard

When seeding `Author`, each `Book`'s `author` back-relation is left `undefined` to prevent infinite recursion. Seeding a `Book` directly works fine and does populate `book.author`.

## seed() builder

`seed(EntityClass)` returns a builder with four methods.

### save / saveMany — persist to database

```ts
import { seed } from '@joakimbugge/typeorm-seeder'

const author = await seed(Author).save({ dataSource })
// author.id    → assigned by the database
// author.books → 3 persisted Book instances

const authors = await seed(Author).saveMany(5, { dataSource })
```

### create / createMany — build without persisting

```ts
const author = await seed(Author).create()
// Plain Author instance — no id, fully populated relations

const books = await seed(Book).createMany(10)
```

### Multiple entity types at once

```ts
const [author, book] = await seed([Author, Book]).create()
// Relations between Author and Book are disabled by default

const [author, book] = await seed([Author, Book]).save({ dataSource, relations: true })
// relations: true enables cross-entity relation seeding
```

`createMany`/`saveMany` return an array per class:

```ts
const [authors, books] = await seed([Author, Book]).createMany(3)
// authors → [Author, Author, Author]
// books   → [Book, Book, Book]
```

### relations option

```ts
const author = await seed(Author).create({ relations: false })
// author.books → undefined (no relation properties set)
```

### values option — override seeded properties

```ts
const booking = await seed(Booking).save({ dataSource, values: { status: 'confirmed' } })

// Per-instance factory in values:
const bookings = await seed(Booking).saveMany(10, {
  dataSource,
  values: {
    price: () => faker.number.float({ min: 10, max: 500 }),
    status: 'confirmed',
  },
})
```

`values` runs after all `@Seed` factories and wins unconditionally. Works on properties with no `@Seed` decorator too.

### Accessing DataSource inside a factory

The `dataSource` you pass in options is forwarded to every factory via `SeedContext`:

```ts
@Seed(async ({ dataSource }) => {
  return dataSource!.getRepository(Role).findOneBy({ name: 'admin' })
})
@ManyToOne(() => Role)
role!: Role
```

## @Seeder and runSeeders

Organize seeding into classes for complex scenarios. Declare dependencies and let the library determine execution order.

```ts
import { Seeder, runSeeders, seed } from '@joakimbugge/typeorm-seeder'
import type { SeederInterface, SeedContext } from '@joakimbugge/typeorm-seeder'

@Seeder()
class UserSeeder implements SeederInterface {
  async run(ctx: SeedContext) {
    await seed(User).saveMany(10, ctx)
  }
}

@Seeder({ dependencies: [UserSeeder] })
class PostSeeder implements SeederInterface {
  async run(ctx: SeedContext) {
    await seed(Post).saveMany(50, ctx)
  }
}

await runSeeders([PostSeeder], { dataSource })
// UserSeeder runs first, then PostSeeder
```

- Dependencies always complete before the seeders that depend on them.
- Seeders with no dependency relationship run **concurrently**.
- A seeder is run at most once, even if declared multiple times.
- Circular dependencies are detected at runtime and throw.

### Returning seeded entities

```ts
const results = await runSeeders([UserSeeder], { dataSource })
const users = results.get(UserSeeder) as User[]
```

### Seeding without @Seed()

Use `values` to inject data at call time instead of on the decorator:

```ts
@Seeder({ dependencies: [UserSeeder] })
class BookingSeeder implements SeederInterface {
  async run({ dataSource }: SeedContext) {
    const users = await dataSource!.getRepository(User).find()
    const user = faker.helpers.arrayElement(users)
    await seed(Booking).saveMany(10, { dataSource, values: { user } })
  }
}
```

## CLI

Install `ts-node` as a dev dependency to use `.ts` files directly.

```bash
# Run all @Seeder classes from a glob pattern
npx @joakimbugge/typeorm-seeder seed:run './src/seeders/*.ts' -d ./src/datasource.ts

# Persist N instances of every entity that has @Seed decorators
npx @joakimbugge/typeorm-seeder seed:entities './src/entities/*.ts' -d ./src/datasource.ts --count 20

# List seeder run history
npx @joakimbugge/typeorm-seeder seed:list -d ./src/datasource.ts

# Remove a seeder from history so it runs again
npx @joakimbugge/typeorm-seeder seed:untrack UserSeeder -d ./src/datasource.ts
```

Pass `--dry-run` (`-n`) to `seed:run` or `seed:entities` to preview without writing to the database.

The DataSource file must export a DataSource as the default export:

```ts
// datasource.ts
import { DataSource } from 'typeorm'
export default new DataSource({ ... })
```

If `-d` is omitted, the CLI looks for `typeorm-seeder.config.ts` then `typeorm-seeder.config.js` in the current directory.

npm scripts shorthand:

```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "seed:run": "typeorm-seeder seed:run './src/seeders/*.ts' -d ./src/datasource.ts"
  }
}
```

## Logging

```ts
// Console logger (console.log for progress, console.warn for failures)
await runSeeders([UserSeeder], { logging: true })

// Delegate to TypeORM's own logger
await runSeeders([UserSeeder], { dataSource, logging: 'typeorm' })

// Custom logger
await runSeeders([UserSeeder], {
  logging: true,
  logger: { log: (msg) => myLogger.info(msg), warn: (msg) => myLogger.warn(msg) },
})
```

## Hooks

```ts
await runSeeders([UserSeeder, PostSeeder], {
  dataSource,
  onBefore: (seeder) => console.log(`Starting ${seeder.name}...`),
  onAfter: (seeder, durationMs) => console.log(`${seeder.name} done in ${durationMs}ms`),
  onError: (seeder, error) => console.error(`${seeder.name} failed`, error),
  skip: (seeder) => alreadyRun.has(seeder.name),  // return true to skip
})
```

Skipped seeders do not trigger `onBefore`, `onAfter`, or `onError`.

## Running seed scripts directly

When running with Node.js, `reflect-metadata` must be the **first** import:

```ts
import 'reflect-metadata'
import { seed } from '@joakimbugge/typeorm-seeder'
```

TypeScript execution — use ts-node, not tsx (tsx strips decorator metadata):

```bash
node --no-warnings --loader ts-node/esm src/seed.ts   # ESM
npx ts-node src/seed.ts                                # CommonJS
```

### Loading entities/seeders from glob patterns

```ts
import { loadEntities, loadSeeders } from '@joakimbugge/seeder'
import { seed, runSeeders } from '@joakimbugge/typeorm-seeder'

const entities = await loadEntities(['dist/entities/**/*.js'])
const seeders = await loadSeeders([UserSeeder, 'dist/seeders/Post*.js'])
await runSeeders(seeders, { dataSource })
```

## Tree entities

All four TypeORM tree strategies work with `@Seed`: `adjacency-list`, `materialized-path`, `closure-table`, `nested-set`.

```ts
@Entity()
@Tree('materialized-path')
class Category {
  @Seed(() => faker.commerce.department())
  @Column()
  name!: string

  @Seed()
  @TreeParent()
  parent?: Category

  @Seed({ count: 2 })
  @TreeChildren()
  children!: Category[]
}

const category = await seed(Category).save({ dataSource })
// category.parent   → one Category (no further parent — circular guard)
// category.children → [Category, Category] (no further children)
```

Depth is limited to one level by the circular guard. For deeper trees, build them manually in a `@Seeder` using the TypeORM tree repository API.

## NestJS integration

Use `@joakimbugge/nest-typeorm-seeder` for NestJS projects. It wraps `runSeeders` in a `SeederModule` that runs seeders on application bootstrap and tracks which have already run.

```ts
import { SeederModule } from '@joakimbugge/nest-typeorm-seeder'

@Module({
  imports: [
    TypeOrmModule.forRoot({ ... }),
    SeederModule.forRoot({ seeders: [UserSeeder] }),
  ],
})
export class AppModule {}
```
